# Overview: the study in brief {#sec-overview}

This chapter is the map of the territory. It states the problem the study
addresses, sketches the approach, and lists the headline findings you will
reproduce — so that when you read the method chapters you always know where a
step is heading.

## The problem

A **child care desert** is a tract where the supply of child care is inadequate
relative to the number of young children. The standard way to draw one is to
compute an access score — here, an enhanced two-step floating catchment area
(E2SFCA) score measuring accessible slots per child — and then apply a threshold:
below $A = 0.33$ slots per child, a tract is a desert.

That binary label is useful and consequential; it steers where public dollars
go. But it hides two things. First, the inputs are uncertain: the count of young
children is an American Community Survey (ACS) estimate with a margin of error,
and the usable capacity of providers is measured with error. Second, a threshold
turns a continuous, uncertain quantity into a hard yes/no that carries no sense
of confidence. A tract just below the line and a tract far below it get the same
label, even though our certainty about them is very different.

This study keeps the access model exactly as it is and asks a sharper question:
**for each tract, how sure are we that it is a desert, and which tracts can we
declare with controlled error?**

## The approach in one paragraph

Hold the E2SFCA design fixed. Propagate the two dominant input uncertainties —
ACS under-five **demand** error and survey-calibrated **capacity** error —
through a Bayesian spatial coverage model: a Besag–York–Mollié (BYM2) model with
a Tweedie likelihood, fit once by INLA. Draw 2,000 **joint posterior samples**
that carry both the model's own uncertainty and the input uncertainties, and read
off each tract's posterior probability of being a desert. Then feed those
probabilities into **declaration procedures** that control the false discovery
rate (FDR) and the false discovery exceedance (FDX) at preregistered levels, with
county decisions weighted by the number of children under five. The result is a
tiered policy product: a defensible core, an extended priority list, and an
explicit agenda for where better data would matter most.

Two design commitments make the study honest and reproducible, and you will see
both enforced in the code:

- **A fixed access model.** Only demand and capacity uncertainty (plus spatial
  smoothing) are propagated; the catchment, decay, and competition design of the
  E2SFCA score is held constant. Uncertainty in that *functional form* is a
  different question and is out of scope (@sec-inputs).
- **Preregistration.** The primary specification — the adequacy standard, the
  error targets, the county weight — is frozen and SHA-256-hashed *before* any
  model result is inspected (@sec-inputs). Alternatives are labeled sensitivity
  analyses and never replace it.

## The setting and the universe

The study covers Alabama at the 2020 census-tract scale: 1,436 tracts across 67
counties. Twenty-seven tracts have a zero ACS under-five estimate and are kept
only for map topology, leaving **1,409 analysis tracts** and **294,417 children
under five**. Supply is a fixed universe of **2,164 licensed providers** with
**117,062 licensed daytime slots**. Demand comes from the ACS 2023 5-year
estimates; capacity is calibrated with four quarterly provider surveys. The
deterministic map that this study extends flags **690** point deserts, with a
**519-tract** "always-desert" stability anchor used later for triangulation.

## The findings you will reproduce

The three research questions organize the method chapters and the exhibits.

**RQ1 — Shortfalls are widespread but unevenly evidenced** (@sec-posterior).
The median tract posterior desert probability is **0.611**, and 816 tracts exceed
a probability of 0.50. But 517 tracts (36.7%) fall in an ambiguous 0.25–0.75
band, and posterior intervals in the high-uncertainty demand stratum are more
than twice as wide as elsewhere (relative width 6.71 vs 3.11).

**RQ2 — Error control reclassifies the map, it does not merely shrink it**
(@sec-declarations). A step-up procedure declares **512** tracts at
$q = 0.10$; a stricter exceedance rule certifies a **412-tract** core. Of the 690
deterministic deserts, **500 survive** (72.5%), **190** lose declarable status
without being exonerated (their median desert probability is still 0.689), and
**12** enter.

**RQ3 — County decisions depend on the estimand; tract declarations are robust**
(@sec-declarations). The child-weighted rule declares **13** of 67 counties;
equal-tract and land-area weights give 9 and 19. Across the preregistered
threshold grids the tract declarations move only with the policy dials, and 85.0%
of the 519-tract stability anchor is FDR-declared.

## What the pipeline looks like

The analysis is eleven numbered steps, grouped into four stages. @sec-getting-started
shows how to run them; the method chapters explain each one.

| Stage | Steps | Chapter |
|---|---|---|
| Inputs and input uncertainty | 0.2, 1.1, 1.2, 1.3 | @sec-inputs |
| The Bayesian spatial model | 2.1 | @sec-model |
| Posterior propagation and desert probability | 2.2, 2.3 | @sec-posterior |
| Error-controlled declarations | 3.1, 3.2, 3.3 | @sec-declarations |
| Figures, tables, and the interactive map | 4.1 | @sec-exhibits |

A single registry, `outputs/key_numbers.csv`, holds every headline value once;
nothing downstream recomputes a number it can look up.
